SEARCH
Module DSA
Package com.dsa.pattern

Class DivideAndConquer

java.lang.Object
com.dsa.pattern.DivideAndConquer
public class DivideAndConquer extends Object
Demonstrates the Divide and Conquer Pattern.

Divide and Conquer is a technique where a problem is divided into smaller subproblems, each subproblem is solved recursively, and the results are combined to form the final solution.

Key Idea

  • Divide the problem into smaller subproblems
  • Solve each subproblem recursively
  • Combine the solutions of subproblems to get the final result

When to Use

  • Sorting algorithms (Merge Sort, Quick Sort)
  • Binary Search
  • Finding maximum/minimum in an array
  • Matrix multiplication (Strassen’s algorithm)

Example Implementation


 package com.dsa.pattern;

 public class DivideAndConquerExample {

     // Merge Sort implementation
     public static void mergeSort(int[] arr, int left, int right) {
         if (left >= right) return; // base case

         int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;

         mergeSort(arr, left, mid);       // divide left half
         mergeSort(arr, mid + 1, right);  // divide right half

         merge(arr, left, mid, right);    // conquer (merge)
     }

     private static void merge(int[] arr, int left, int mid, int right) {

         int n1 = mid - left + 1;
         int n2 = right - mid;

         int[] L = new int[n1];
         int[] R = new int[n2];

         for (int i = 0; i < n1; i++) L[i] = arr[left + i];
         for (int i = 0; i < n2; i++) R[i] = arr[mid + 1 + i];

         int i = 0, j = 0, k = left;
         while (i < n1 && j < n2) {
             if (L[i] <= R[j]) arr[k++] = L[i++];
             else arr[k++] = R[j++];
         }

         while (i < n1) arr[k++] = L[i++];
         while (j < n2) arr[k++] = R[j++];
     }

     public static void main(String[] args) {

         int[] arr = {5, 2, 9, 1, 5, 6};

         mergeSort(arr, 0, arr.length - 1);

         System.out.println("Sorted Array:");
         for (int num : arr) System.out.print(num + " ");
     }
 }

Complexity

  • Time Complexity: O(n log n)
  • Space Complexity: O(n) auxiliary space for merge

Applications

  • Merge Sort / Quick Sort
  • Binary Search
  • Finding maximum/minimum in arrays
  • Strassen’s Matrix Multiplication
  • Divide-and-Conquer algorithms in geometry or DP
